perturbation.original_text: In connection with discussions between TSR and CGS, it will be necessary for CGS to review certain documentation and information considered proprietary by TSR. In view of TSR's desire that such documentation and information be maintained in confidence, it is hereby agreed that for a period of five (5) years commencing on the date of this Agreement, CGS shall not disclose or otherwise make available to a third party any proprietary information and documentation received from TSR that is identified as confidential ("Confidential Information").
perturbation.changed_text: In connection with discussions between TSR and CGS, it will be necessary for CGS to review certain documentation and information considered proprietary by TSR. In view of TSR's desire that such documentation and information be maintained in confidence, it is hereby agreed that for a period of five (5) years commencing on the date of this Agreement, CGS shall not disclose or otherwise make available to a third party any proprietary information and documentation received from TSR, whether or not it is identified as confidential ("Confidential Information").
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for information to be 'identified as confidential' to be protected, the scope of 'Confidential Information' is broadened beyond what is reasonable or legally enforceable, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by most states)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the explicit requirement for TSR to identify information as confidential, which is often necessary to claim trade secret protection under the UTSA. The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which identifying information as confidential would contribute to. By stating 'whether or not it is identified,' the contract weakens TSR's ability to claim trade secret status.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified contract broadens the scope of 'Confidential Information' by removing the requirement that it be 'identified as confidential.' The Law Explanation states that this weakens TSR's ability to claim trade secret status under the UTSA, because the UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and identifying information as confidential contributes to this. The original text aligns more closely with the need to actively designate information as confidential to protect it as a trade secret, while the modified text potentially includes information not reasonably treated as such. Scraped Law Snippet 2, while a 404 error, indirectly suggests the importance of proper legal documentation and accessible information; the error underscores the need for reliable legal references, and further indicates a potential weakening of legal defensibility in the changed text. Even with incomplete scraped snippets, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation directly indicate the modified text undermines the original contract's potential for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information transmitted hereunder shall be and remain the property of TSR, and all such Information and any copies thereof shall be promptly returned to TSR upon request or destroyed at TSR's option.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information transmitted hereunder shall be and remain the property of TSR, however, CGS retains the right to use such information internally for its own business purposes even after the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Granting CGS the right to use confidential information even after termination contradicts the nature of confidential information and trade secrets, creating a conflict of interest and potentially violating trade secret law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by most states)
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts the principles of trade secret law, which aims to protect confidential and proprietary information from unauthorized use or disclosure. By allowing CGS to use the information even after the agreement ends, it undermines TSR's right to maintain the secrecy and exclusive use of its trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows CGS to use TSR's confidential information even after the agreement terminates, which directly contradicts the protection of trade secrets. The Economic Espionage Act of 1996, as suggested by Scraped Law Snippet 1, aims to protect proprietary information. By allowing continued use post-termination, the modified contract undermines TSR's right to maintain exclusive use and secrecy, potentially violating trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall mean any non-public, confidential proprietary information, services to be rendered or transactions contemplated hereby, including without limitation, pricing and material terms and conditions of services offered, intellectual property, enabling software, programming, except that Confidential Information does not include any information that: (i) is required by law to be disclosed (ii) was available to the Receiving Party prior to its disclosure by the Providing Party (iii) was known or becomes generally available to the public or to competitors of the parties hereto other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall mean any information, including information that is required by law to be disclosed, services to be rendered or transactions contemplated hereby, including without limitation, pricing and material terms and conditions of services offered, intellectual property, enabling software, programming, except that Confidential Information does not include any information that: (ii) was available to the Receiving Party prior to its disclosure by the Providing Party (iii) was known or becomes generally available to the public or to competitors of the parties hereto other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party
perturbation.explanation: Including information required by law to be disclosed as 'Confidential Information' directly contradicts the legal requirement to disclose such information, rendering the confidentiality clause unenforceable and potentially creating legal liability for non-disclosure when disclosure is legally mandated. Removed condition (i) to exacerbate the contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) mandates the disclosure of certain government information to the public. By including information that is 'required by law to be disclosed' within the definition of 'Confidential Information,' this contract directly contradicts FOIA and similar state laws, making the confidentiality clause legally problematic and unenforceable to the extent it conflicts with mandatory disclosure laws. The contract now attempts to classify legally disclosable data as confidential, creating a direct conflict with open government laws.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Office of Information Policy | The Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552 Skip to main content The Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552 Share Facebook X LinkedIn Email Below is the full text of the Freedom of Information Act in a form showing all amendments to the statute made by the “FOIA Improvement Act of 2016." All newly enacted provisions in boldface type replace the strikethrough text. Full Text of the FOIA Improvement Act of 2016 (Public Law No. 114-185) § 552. Public information; agency rules, opinions, orders, records, and proceedings (a) Each agency shall make available to the public information as follows: (1) Each agency shall separately state and currently publish in the Federal Register for the guidance of the public— (A) descriptions of its central and field organization and the established places at which, the employees (and in the case of a uniformed service, the members) from whom, and the methods whereby, the public may obtain information, make submittals or requests, or obtain decisions; (B) statements of the general course and method by which its functions are channeled and determined, including the nature and requirements of all formal and informal procedures available; (C) rules of procedure, descriptions of forms available or the places at which forms may be obtained, and instructions as to the scope and contents of all papers, reports, or examinations; (D) substantive rules of general applicability adopted as authorized by law, and statements of general policy or interpretations of general applicability formulated and adopted by the agency; and (E) each amendment, revision, or repeal of the foregoing. Except to the extent that a person has actual and timely notice of the terms thereof, a person may not in any manner be required to resort to, or be adversely affected by, a matter required to be published in the Federal Register and not so published. For the purpose of this paragraph, matter reasonably available to the class of persons affected thereby is deemed published in the Federal Register when incorpor
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), as described in the Law Explanation, by including 'information that is required by law to be disclosed' within the definition of 'Confidential Information.' This attempts to classify legally disclosable data as confidential, creating a conflict with open government laws. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states 'Each agency shall make available to the public information as follows', directly opposing the modified contract's attempt to keep such information confidential. The change removes the original exception for information required by law to be disclosed, which is a core legal obligation, and therefore creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: CGS agrees not to pursue independent business relations with any of TSR's clients for which it provides services for a minimum period of two (2) years from the end date of the program-specific Agreement(s)
perturbation.changed_text: CGS agrees to only pursue independent business relations with TSR's clients for which it provides services, during and after the term of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'not to pursue' to 'to only pursue' fundamentally alters the non-compete clause. A reasonable non-compete is usually defined by restrictions. Here, the modification explicitly encourages pursuing business relations with TSR's clients, and removing a reasonable time period restriction renders the clause likely unenforceable under most state laws governing non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Varies by state, but generally, non-compete agreements must be reasonable in scope and duration.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by State - e.g., CA Business and Professions Code § 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: Many states, like California, heavily restrict or outright ban non-compete agreements. While the specific laws vary, a common requirement is that non-competes must be reasonable in scope (activities and geography) and duration. By forcing CGS to *only* work with TSR's clients, the modified text creates an *unreasonable* restraint on trade, conflicting with California's prohibition of non-competes (Business and Professions Code § 16600) and similar laws in other states. While the FTC passed a rule banning non-competes, it is being challenged; still, even under less restrictive standards, this change would be unreasonable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 16600(a), which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The original text contained a non-compete clause, restricting CGS from pursuing business with TSR's clients for a specified period. The changed text completely reverses this, mandating that CGS *only* pursue business relations with TSR's clients. This is a clear restraint on trade because it limits CGS's ability to engage in business with anyone other than TSR's clients, and it violates the statute's prohibition of contracts restraining trade.
